project: The poem chronicles a single evening and morning of communal living, using precise domestic details to map the rhythm of shared meals, conversation, and quiet intimacy among friends in an urban apartment.
title: Just Enough Freedom
body:
A shared flat,
nine storeys up,
with a view of the city.

The porch faces south—
    the Scenic Rim on the horizon,
    city streets close by,
    blocks of flats, a park,
    the Brisbane River between,
        close enough to touch;
    no morning sun,
        but a couch,
        cushioned seats,
        and a coffee table—
            a porch built for six.


But the city is quiet, still—
no cars, no motion, no sound
    rising from the street,
lights in flats flickering
as people carry on
    with their evening.

We stay where we are.


Guests gather again in the kitchen—
a couple visiting from Adelaide,
two Sydney students on break,
the host puts Tom Waits
on portable speakers.

We wash vegetables, rinse rice,
open cans of bamboo shoots,
dice fresh chicken thighs.
Some cook, others put out
plates, chopsticks, forks,
and knives.

Glasses are set on the bench.
Wine is opened, sodas and beers.

Through the kitchen window,
lights still flicker in flats,
utes remain parked,
Mount Barney holds.


We finish, plate, and take our meals
to the porch, arrange, rearrange.
The couch is full of conversation.
Those in chairs laugh. Wine is poured.
I teach a student how to use chopsticks.

Thai curry chicken on rice.
A Margaret River cabernet.
Garlic butter naan.

Yellow candlelight replaces
the white of incandescent bulbs.


I carry plates to the kitchen.
Steve puts on Courtney Barnett.
Anne reaches for someone’s hand.

They all dance.
I wash.
Claire dries.


Outside, Victoria Bridge is empty.
Claire takes more wine and we join.
Courtney is replaced by The National.
    We sing and dance.
Anne and I head for the porch. We smoke.
Loud music fails to disturb the city.

We go back in. I dance with Claire.
Night turns to morning.
Morning leads to bed.

We wake late.
Press coffee on the porch.
Slice bread.
Arrange, rearrange sliced devon.

Below us the cars start to move.
Street life reaches us again.
Victoria Bridge is no longer empty.
Mount Cordeaux is close enough to touch.

The city moves again.


We stand, take
    our French press,
    coffee cups,
    light cream
        to the kitchen
            and begin to cook.
notes: The porch looks south from a ninth-floor Brisbane flat, with Mount Barney and the Scenic Rim visible beyond the river. Friends gather for an evening that stretches into morning, cooking and sharing meals while the city below stays unusually quiet. The stillness outside mirrors the contained world they've created together, nine storeys up.

project: The poem constructs an architectural interior that functions as an anatomical metaphor, using precise structural details to map the hidden mechanisms and gradual deterioration of a living body.
title: Load-Bearing Interior
body:
It is not empty here.

The room begins in a narrow corridor,
walls close and damp,
where a steady drip measures
the distance between surfaces.

Beyond that,
it opens.

Columns rise without ornament,
pale and load-bearing,
holding up a ceiling
that presses downward
without ever touching.

The air is thick,
slow to circulate.
Light does not enter directly.
It filters through layers,
arriving thinned,
arriving altered,
as if it had passed through
too many hands.

Along the perimeter,
there are smaller chambers—
alcoves no wider than a shoulder,
lined in soft material
that gives
and gives
and does not recover.

Pipes run through the walls.
Some pulse.
Some have gone still.
A faint current hums in the distance,
irregular,
then absent.

In the center,
the floor has lifted slightly,
a quiet swelling
beneath intact tile.
It does not announce itself.
It simply changes the level
on which everything stands.

The ceiling adjusts in response.
Beams strain invisibly.
Hairline fractures map themselves
across plaster,
fine as capillaries.

There are no windows.

If there were,
they would look out
onto another structure
pressing close,
its weight leaning inward.

The room expands
where it can.
It narrows
where it must.

Nothing in it is decorative.
Every surface carries something.

When the current stops entirely,
the air does not clear.
The columns remain upright.
The lifted floor does not settle.

The structure holds
what it has made.
notes: This is architecture as anatomy, where corridors and chambers form the interior geography of a body under pressure. The load-bearing columns and swelling floor create a space that breathes without windows, pulses without decoration, and holds its weight through invisible strain.

project: The poem tracks a moment of charged silence between two people, explicitly rejecting metaphorical language in favor of direct physical observation and psychological pressure.
title: Not a Metaphor
body:
I rehearse a line I think is clever
    while the kettle clicks off.

She leans in the doorway
    and clever dies first.

My breathing goes wrong.
Not poetic-wrong—
    asthma wrong, like misjudging
        a staircase.

Her arm rests on the counter.
A faint knife scar.
I don’t ask about it.
    I track it.
        Already ashamed.

The room begins to pair things:
        her silence with my name,
        the hum of the fridge with waiting.

She barely moves.
Neither do I.
The space between us thickens,
    visible as dust in sunlight.

She almost says something—
    stops.
The almost
        lands heavier.

I think of a river and immediately hate myself.
Her knuckle taps the counter twice.
        Enough.

What I expect to end
    doesn’t.
My attention keeps sliding forward,
    past manners,
    past restraint.

I am no longer deciding
        what this is.

I try, once more,
    to make her stand
        for anything else.
She exhales.
The idea collapses.

Nothing is crossed out.
I stop reaching for language
    and stay with the pressure of her presence,
    unsymbolised,
    unforgiving.
    Close enough
        that even silence
            is contact.
notes: The poem began as an argument with myself about what relationships are allowed to mean — and whether I was actually present, or already composing.

project: The poem adopts the perspective of cancerous cells describing their growth and spread through a body, presenting malignancy as a neutral process of expansion without moral judgment.
title: Unchecked
body:
I began as a softness
in a place already full.

There was warmth,
a steady wash of iron,
corridors of red
opening and closing
without decision.

I learned the walls by touch.

They yielded.
They tightened.
They thinned.

Where there was current,
I followed it.
Where there was stillness,
I pressed until it altered.

Division was not ambition.
It was repetition.
A quiet doubling
that felt like continuity.

The larger body dimmed gradually.

Signals that once arrived
in bright pulses
came irregularly,
then not at all.
The rooms cooled.
The rivers slowed.

I did not register alarm.
Only difference.

Space narrowed.
Then widened strangely,
as if boundaries had forgotten
their instructions.

At the end,
there was no verdict.

Only the absence
of resistance.

I would have continued.
notes: This is a voice from inside a body that is failing, speaking from within the very process that is causing that failure.

project: The poem adopts a distant, elevated perspective to reframe cancer as part of natural biological processes, systematically stripping away human emotional language in favor of architectural and mechanical metaphors.
title: Weather Over Brief Structures
body:
From this height, the bodies are temporary scaffolds.

They rise in soft light,
assemble their corridors,
thread themselves with current,
believing the architecture will hold.

It does, for a while.

Inside each one,
divisions occur with devotion—
walls doubling,
chambers extending,
small obediences to instruction.

They call some of these errors.

From here,
there is no error.
Only pattern.

Cells lean into their mandate.
They copy.
They press.
They fill available dark.

When the larger structure weakens,
it is not betrayal.
It is density exceeding frame.

You would name it invasion.
You would name it tragedy.
You would circle brightness on a screen
and give it weight beyond geometry.

From this altitude,
brightness is simply concentration.

The body labors to remain singular.
It seals, cuts, burns,
revises its own blueprints.
Sometimes the revision succeeds.
Sometimes the beams soften.

Neither outcome alters the field.

What you call a life
is a brief coherence of matter,
a gathering
of signal and warmth.

What you call a loss
is the redistribution
of that coherence.

Nothing is punished.
Nothing is spared.

The smallest cluster
of multiplying cells
and the tallest standing figure
share the same duration.

Both lean toward increase.
Both exceed themselves.

From here,
the effort is luminous
and without exception.
notes: The perspective here is aerial and clinical, observing cellular division and bodily processes from a detached vantage point. The voice shifts between biological observation and human interpretation, questioning how we name and understand the processes of growth, illness, and mortality. What appears as tragedy from ground level transforms into pattern when viewed from sufficient distance.
